## [00:00 - 03:01]

Here's where we ended this session yesterday, street smart copywriting secrets. And I covered this one, how to double your odds of success, and you can even triple them if you want to test three panels. We've seen how hard it is to predict a winner. So if you have a great other idea, especially one if it's even more creative, but therefore riskier than your first one, it's really a good idea to do another package or add. Even if it's, even if the interior or the body copy is still the same. And if you can do three and your client, or if you're other client, you can have the budget to test three, we can see how hard it is to pick the winner. So it really, in effect, doubles or triples your odds. The more test panels you can, worthy of testing, that you throw into the mix. And two, after your research stage, write at least 20 to 25 good headlines. I've always done that, and I always hate doing it because it's a lot of work. And the standard that I use, it has to really be a good headline. It can't just be going through this as an exercise to turn out 20 to 25 mediocre headlines. You want to shoot for 20 to 25. And I promise you, if you go through this exercise, your 17th or 22nd will be a lot better than your first or second. No matter how good you think that one is, don't fall in love with it and think you've got it. Just let your mind over the next several days come up with new combinations, perhaps combine a great headline with a different idea in the subhead and then maybe switch, make the subhead idea the headline. You can create great combinations going. But I do promise you that if you go through that exercise, you'll love the headlines at the end of the process much more than the headlines at the beginning. Number three, start your body copy by paying off your headline. This is a very, very common mistake. And I know as a copy chief, I used to see this all the time from the writers in my department, where the copywriter and copy chief in the staff and sometimes the client gets involved in writing a brilliant headline. And that's sort of like one separate job. And then they go off and the copywriter writes the body copy, kind of forgetting about the first job that they just did with the headline. And they go off in a totally different direction that never references the headline. That's so common. If you look in many ads, you'll see that. And the reason is because the jobs are done separately. So the first rule that I would apply when reviewing somebody's copy is, well, I guess the first one is do I like the overall concept, the headline and visual combination and the format and so on. But then after that, after Dear Friend or Dear Godner or whatever, who you're writing to, there's this body copy pick up instantly off the headline. You don't want any speed bump, any dislocation. You have a nice railroad track and the locomotive is coming off that headline. If you change total directions, you are derailing the locomotive that just spent so much time in creating your headline. So it's a very common mistake and you must watch for that. Don't address your readers as a group, such as America, friends, or all of you, or folks,

## [03:01 - 06:04]

or anything else. Not so common, but we all tend to do this because it's that same symptom as thinking as the marketer, thinking as the Ford executives inside the Ford building. Well, we're inside the building. We're writing to all the folks out there. I remember seeing a commercial for a Chevy Blazer and the announcer said, come on, America, let's get out and discover that Chevy Blazer. I kind of looked over my shoulder and said, is America here? Am I America? Where's America? I don't think of myself as America. They're just not talking to me at all. Inside the building makes a lot of sense. We're going to get this message out across America. Come on, America, discover the Chevy Blazer. Makes no sense at all. Nobody registers with that kind of an address. Number five. However, in space advertising, it pays to address the readers of the publication. If you're running an ad in the New York Times, it really helps to say a special offer to readers of the New York Times. Without the New York Times permission, you've sort of gotten almost like a joint venture endorsement. A very nice little tip. I've never seen it not boost response. If you could work that in space also, a special invitation to subscribers of the Wall Street Journal or whoever's, if you can personalize that to a list, if the list is big enough, I can pay very nicely. Or your house list. You want to acknowledge whatever you can that you know about your readers because it flatters them rather than just being a stranger. Number six. Copyrighters love curiosity headlines, such as, and I've written my share of these until the marketplace sort of beat it out of me because it just almost never worked. This is one that I just happened to pull from my file. I have a collection of curiosity headlines. Most people with the most copy chiefs and copyrights would love this headline. This is an ad about Manusel Cigars and his business is really taking off. Everybody loves ads like this, but over time, one of the best copy chiefs I've ever worked for did a study of this and he said that curiosity headlines, where you really don't even know what the product is selling, win only about one quarter of the time, no matter how compelling they are compared with benefit-driven headlines. So you automatically get a 75% edge if there's an understandable benefit in your headline. You can buck those odds and when you do, you very often have a blockbuster. But just know that you're going against the odds by 75%. This is a classic benefit headline by, I think, an energy company on how to cut your heating bills more than one third. It's hard not to, especially with fuel costs very high. Somebody sees that it could probably be improved by making it look more like a real article, but somebody worried about their fuel cost, I think, would be caught by that headline and read the ad. Here's another one, how to add 20 to 40 pins to your bowling average, guaranteed. A die-hard bowler, that's a hard ad to ignore, pure benefit. Another one, laugh at the cold, thermal underwear. I see this ad running year after year after year. An ad from my all-time classic file is at four and a half, she's reading third grade

books and I love the visual there because it shows a little girl just reaching up higher than she really is, which is the whole concept behind the course. Xerox was in the education business, they decided to get into that business. I think they're out of that business, as well as many other businesses they jumped into. Number eight, for strongest results of all, combined benefits and curiosity. This is where curiosity can really work well for you if you make sure to include a benefit and maybe some proof around it. For example, there's a package I wrote that is very successful for Jeff Greenberg, a newsletter about succeeding as an executive and we created a premium called the Black Book of Office Politics and the headline of this magazine style ad was, Secrets from the Black Book of Office Politics. That Black Book always tends to increase curiosity and in this case the subject was greatly provoked curiosity about relationships in the office, who stabs who behind the back, things like that. This is one of the best packages they ever wrote. It was for Jim Blanchard a number of years ago, had a newsletter written by two sisters named the Aidan sisters. These were two women who went to Costa Rica and went to work for an investor, a very wealthy secretive investor in Costa Rica who needed some office help and they went to, and they were very bright women, and they went to work for him and they absorbed all of his brilliant and very secretive ways of making a lot of money from the markets. They later left his employ and with the knowledge that they gained from him started producing their own newsletter and they took the financial world by storm because it's such a, most of that world even today is dominated by men and to have these young attractive women be making incredibly accurate predictions and we cited all of the places, like the very major media that were just a gawg that these two young women could have the knowledge to be outperforming the top Wall Street analysts and they were and this package was just a blockbuster. Because of the curiosity factor, people don't normally think of Wall Street Hanchos as coming from Costa Rica and being too young attractive women, it's much more of a male dominated Wall Street world so this added a lot of interest. This is a very useful secret. To defeat a killer objection, your best strategy is to attack it head on in your headline.

## [09:10 - 12:12]

Very often you'll have a product that you're asked to sell. If you're a professional copywriter and like Captain John Raid you've got to go out and catch fish even when the fish aren't biting and on rainy days and days when it's, the fishing is no fun and sometimes you'll be given an assignment that's no fun and you think how can I ever sell this because the objection is so strong. For example, selling shoes by mail, how can you purchase shoes by mail when you can't try them on? So the best way to attack an anticipated prospect objection in those situations is to put it right into your headline. By shoes by mail you have to be kidding. So that combines, it states what the company does but right away gets the objection that the prospect will have into the headline and so the prospect then has to feel, well, what is the answer to this? And then the body copy, you probably can't read it but it goes on to say, look, when you try shoes on in a shoe store, you walk around for a minute or two, you really don't get a feeling for how you normally wear shoes. With our company, we'll let you use them for a week, a month, whatever you want and if they're not comfortable, after that length of time where you normally wear shoes, send them back and complete refund. So that's why how they answered the objection in the ad and it was very effective. Built a whole business around it. So you can cut and paste these things but it's much better if your prospects really do think you're crazy for doing something. But don't think you have to be a publisher or a major advertiser to put these ideas into practice. They work for any business because our prospects are all alike. They want a promise that relates to them. They want a problem that they're having, a proof and a proposition. That goes across all product categories. Another principle that goes across all categories is when in doubt, sell information. Some people have come up to me and asked, well, what do I do? I have a commodity product. Mayther, they had the Shell account, and what's more of a commodity product than gasoline? Or another one, the breakdown book, exactly what to do if you break down and how to prepare for such a situation. So they counted all the multiple visits that came in from people to get these booklets, and then while they were there, they filled up with Shell. You can do this with any product. ## [18:18 - 21:20]

We never knew. When you send out a health mailing, you're never sure, should I reach, should I try to reach everybody with these various conditions? Should I aim just at the people over 50? And we have found through a lot of testing that it's always good to aim at a dead center. And unless that dead center is seven people, if it's a passionate group, you're always better off aiming at the passionate group. You don't want to intentionally exclude the people on the periphery. But it's almost like a political campaign. If you don't have your really core, passionate troops with you, you're not going to win. So your best strategy is always aim for the center of your market. For example, in a food newsletter, we might always be sure that we are aiming for die-hard foodies. And if other people want to discover the best foods in the world who are not such die-hard foodies, well, they can come in too. But we want to be sure we write our copy to die-hard foodies. Not leave them out. Because very often you'll have a question, gee, if I broaden this and not use the lingo or the people who are most passionate in the center of the market, and the periphery might not understand my lingo, go with the lingo or the people who are in the core of the market because they're the ones who are going to make or break the mailing or campaign. For example, this is maybe this is sort of another pick that went a contest, one that I flunked when I started work at Dan Roswell's agency. This was a book by an author named Percy Greaves. I would have never thought the one on, it seems so arcane. But see, the heart of that market, the laissez-faire economics, that's almost, it's a very passionate school of economic thinking. It's a laissez-faire school of thinking. And Ludwig von Mises, the one who's giving the testimonial there, is a hero of that group. And I would have never known that. I just thought, well, yeah, I'd like to know some basic things about why and how capitalism works. But that, the one on the right, one by a landslide margin, and it was because it was so well aimed at the core group of people who believe in laissez-faire economics. And when I asked the people in charge of the product, I said, you know, does financial information change because there's a different gender involved? It didn't seem to make sense to me. But we had a female editor. We thought, well, maybe a woman talking to another woman about a woman's concerns might really be a good thing, a good way to have women who do not traditionally subscribe to financial newsletters to bring many more of them into the marketplace. But it didn't work. Most of these lists were married couples. And in most of those families, it was the man who took care of the finance. And so we tried to write off the house list to Mrs. So-and-So. But since the man of the house was usually the one subscribing, when this male came in, she just kind of gave it to him or just threw it away. So that did not work. We were trying to educate people into wanting something that they should have, which is a big mistake. And it's easy to sell to people who know you. This should be obvious. But most of our mail, when it goes out, as the sender, are unknown to the person. We're starting at a great disadvantage. So if you can sort of join venture with somebody like a Lou Ruchheiser, he's known. He's like an Uncle Lou to many people who are in the financial community. If you write on his coattails, you get right in the front door and you can sell your product. So always look for people who can either join venture or as an editor of a product, or somebody who can give you an endorsement or give a premium along with your offer that the marketplace knows and loves. Avoid marketing challenges. Number 14, this is so true. It's very much like as an investing. Where many people think that good investors like Warren Buffett and Peter Lynch, they're such great risk takers. I mean, wow, what a world they live in. They sort of make bets on billions of dollars on different company stocks. ## [24:22 - 27:23]

We're great risk avoiders. We want to go to any length to squeeze out all the risk. And then when we have something that looks almost like a sure shot, we will take 100 of those and maybe pick two that look the surest. So don't think that there's a great macho spirit of conquering when you're asked to sell something with a substantial risk. There's risk in there for a reason. And if you want to compound your wealth in marketing the way Warren Buffett and Peter Lynch do it in investing, avoid risk. If you want to squeeze out the risk as much as you can. Don't be seduced by the challenge. We're going to sell ice to Eskimos or a sand in Saudi Arabia. It's just not going to happen. Number 15, to write great headlines, collect great headlines. If you want to collect great headlines, read the reader's digest. It helps greatly in seeing how they will take a rather long and sometimes boring article, condense it down, and make it very interesting. Same for the National Enquirer. People turn up their nose at the National Enquirer. I always get that. It gives me a chuckle. And the headline writers there must make a lot of money because the headlines are very hard to beat. In fact, what I would recommend is have someone cover up with a piece of paper, all of the headlines in, as I say, the reader's digest or National Enquirer or any newspaper that comes up with great headlines. Cover up, have somebody, or if you're not going to cover up the headline yourself, turn the page upside down or something. So you can't read the headline. Read the story and see what headline you would come up with for that article. And then lift up the piece of paper and see how poorly you could fare against what these headline writers do, because they're really, really good. Okay, that's problem, here's a typical promise. Double your closet space in minutes and it shows a little diagram of how they do it for you. I see that ad repeated very often. Or three, if you have great proof elements, you might lead off your headline with the proof elements. Your prospect is gonna have to go through all four of those steps no matter what. For example, free fuel for a year, sold a lot of jet planes. And I would especially urge many of the people in the traditional mass market publishing companies to try different offers. I think that's the least cultivated field where I think improvement can happen. Because we traditionally, as copywriters, especially those of us who've been on royalties for many years, we almost don't look at the offer because we don't, for two reasons. Number one, we don't want to take a chance that our copy was much better, but our offer somehow we guessed it wrong. And just because of that one little slip at the end, we lost the contest after maybe months of work. So we tend to want to just let our copy and design be the only variable tested, not a different offer. But the other reason is that if I went to great lengths to research offers and came up with a brilliant offer, I don't think there's any way I can protect that as a copywriter. Because then, even if it was a big winner, then all the other copywriters competing against me on that product would just slap that offer on the back of their copy. ## [30:29 - 33:32]

Just grab me. I left it on my brother's shirt. This is something I don't know if it would apply to a lot of you, and maybe it would wear out, but it's been proven over and over and over. And I told them all you have to do is put a 30-day hold. Tell them you won't cash the check for 30 days or that you won't process the credit card for 30 days. I worked with another guy who was a publisher, and he published a book on how to become a mail-over-a-millionaire, but he didn't know how to become a mail-over-a-millionaire because he mostly sold to his books through retail outlets.
